findings.title: Language identifier fence stripping matches JSON-variant prefixes incorrectly
findings.problem:
The condition `if inner[:4].lower() == "json"` matches any JSON-variant language identifier like jsonl, json5, jsonschema as a prefix. When the code strips exactly 4 characters for these cases, it leaves remnants (e.g., 'l
', '5
', 'schema
') that fail direct JSON parsing and force fallback to the balanced-brace extractor.
findings.best_practice_violated: Robust text parsing must handle language identifier word boundaries. The fence language tag should match exactly 'json' followed by whitespace or end-of-string, not as a prefix of longer identifiers.
findings.proposed_fix: Change line 706 from `if inner[:4].lower() == "json":` to `if inner.lower().startswith("json") and (len(inner) == 4 or inner[4] in '\n\r \t'):` to correctly handle language identifiers of any length while requiring word boundary separation.

findings.title: Silent parameter contract violation in _attempt routing
findings.problem: The _attempt() function accepts a json_mode parameter (line 460) but does not pass it to _t3_complete() for Tier 3 backends (line 463). The parameter is silently ignored for T3, violating the implicit contract that function parameters should have documented purpose or usage.
findings.best_practice_violated: Function signatures should not accept unused parameters without explicit documentation. Accepted parameters should either be used or clearly marked as ignored in docstring or code comment to prevent maintenance confusion.
findings.proposed_fix: Add a comment to line 463 documenting that json_mode is not passed to T3 because Tier 3 (frontier Claude) relies on prompt instructions and balanced-brace extraction rather than Ollama-style constrained JSON decoding. Consider adding this clarification to the _attempt() docstring as well.

findings.title: OpenTelemetry context not propagated in _run_sync pool submission
findings.problem: When _run_sync submits a coroutine to the thread pool to run in a worker thread (when asyncio.get_running_loop() detects an active loop), the current OpenTelemetry context is not propagated to the worker thread. This causes T3 (frontier Claude) spans from async contexts to lose their trace context and not nest properly under parent spans, breaking trace correlation and making debugging harder.
findings.best_practice_violated: OpenTelemetry context propagation across thread boundaries (mirrored in _with_timeout lines 194-201 for T2). Context must be explicitly captured and attached when crossing thread boundaries in OpenTelemetry-instrumented code.
findings.proposed_fix: Wrap the lambda in _run_sync with context propagation, similar to _with_timeout: capture otel_context.get_current() before the lambda, then attach it in the worker thread before running asyncio.run(coro), and detach after completion. This ensures T3 spans nest under the parent span even when called from within a running event loop.

findings.title: Incorrect advisory lock release semantics in comment
findings.problem: The comment states 'The advisory lock is session-scoped and auto-releases when the connection returns to the pool, so swallowing here is safe'. This is factually incorrect. PostgreSQL advisory locks are session-scoped but the session continues when a connection is returned to the pool. The lock does NOT auto-release; it persists on the reusable connection. If pg_advisory_unlock() fails and the exception is swallowed, the lock remains held. When the same connection is reused, the next caller attempting to acquire the lock will block indefinitely.
findings.best_practice_violated: Accurate documentation of lock semantics; clear explanation of edge cases and failure modes
findings.proposed_fix: Update the comment: 'The advisory lock persists when the connection is returned to the pool because the session continues. If pg_advisory_unlock() fails (rare), the lock remains held. If the same connection is reused and another thread tries to acquire the lock, it will block. This is acceptable because: (1) unlock failures are extremely rare, (2) setup() runs once per process per conn_str, (3) the lock eventually releases when the connection closes. Future maintainers should be aware of this edge case.'

findings.title: Race condition in instrument_langchain() — non-atomic check-and-set on _PROVIDER
findings.problem: The function claims idempotency (docstring lines 91-92) but uses an unprotected check-then-set pattern on the global _PROVIDER variable. If two threads call instrument_langchain() simultaneously before _PROVIDER is assigned, both will pass the check at line 97 and proceed to execute lines 100-142 in parallel, causing: (1) duplicate calls to phoenix.otel.register() (line 115), (2) duplicate calls to LangChainInstrumentor().instrument() (line 134) resulting in duplicate span instrumentation, (3) duplicate atexit.register() calls (line 141), (4) duplicate SIGTERM handler registrations (line 142). This violates the documented idempotency contract and produces observable defects (duplicate spans).
findings.best_practice_violated: Thread-safe singleton pattern; atomic check-and-set (double-checked locking or lock-based idempotency); OpenTelemetry instrumentation idempotency
findings.proposed_fix: Protect the check-and-set with a threading.Lock. Example: `_provider_lock = threading.Lock()` at module level, then wrap lines 96-144 with `with _provider_lock:` to make the entire check-register-cache operation atomic. This ensures only one thread executes the registration code even if multiple threads call instrument_langchain() simultaneously.

findings.title: Hard-prerequisite deferred path cannot be resumed via resume_run()
findings.problem: Gate 2's hard-prerequisite failure path writes a deferred decision to gate_status but does NOT fire an interrupt(). When prerequisites are unmet (e.g., exploratory_complete=False), the gate defers without calling _run_gate, so no interrupt point is created. The run finishes execution normally and routes to END. Later, when the operator wants to retry after meeting prerequisites, resume_run() cannot work because it requires a parked interrupt point. The code comment says 'The operator re-triggers once prereqs are met' but provides no mechanism for this re-trigger - the hard-prereq deferred decision is a dead end.
findings.best_practice_violated: Interrupt/resume safety - deferred decisions should be pausable/resumable. ADR-0015 gate safety mechanism requires operator recovery paths for all gate decisions.
findings.proposed_fix: Gate 2's hard-prerequisite path should fire an interrupt before deferring, allowing the operator to acknowledge the failure via resume_run(). Replace lines 238-272 with a call to _run_gate that presents the hard-failure rationale to the operator as a non-approvable proposal, similar to how validation errors in _run_gate re-prompt the operator. This gives a proper recovery path.

findings.title: Gate 3 docstring claims hard prerequisite but implementation skips validation
findings.problem: Gate 3's docstring (lines 315-316) states 'Hard prerequisite: exploratory_complete must be True', but the implementation immediately calls _run_gate(state, 'gate_3', proposal) without checking this prerequisite. In contrast, Gate 2 (lines 229-236) explicitly validates data_split_verified, exploratory_complete, and hypothesis_set before calling _run_gate, and defers if any are unmet. Gate 3 has no such guard, so it can fire an interrupt asking the operator to review EDA findings when EDA hasn't actually completed. This creates a logic gap: the operator could be presented with a proposal referencing non-existent P1-P5 artifacts.
findings.best_practice_violated: Completeness - documented hard prerequisites should be enforced via code. Pass-3 #217 established that Gate 2 enforces exploratory_complete + hypothesis_set checks; Gate 3 should follow the same pattern for consistency and correctness.
findings.proposed_fix: Add explicit prerequisite validation to gate_3_node before calling _run_gate, matching the pattern from gate_2_node (lines 228-272). Check that exploratory_complete is True (or all agents are in stub_pending for dry-run mode). If prerequisites are unmet, write a deferred gate_status and return, or call _run_gate with a non-approvable proposal indicating the prerequisite failure. This ensures the documented prerequisite is actually enforced.

findings.title: T3 timeout thread loses OTel trace context
findings.problem: _run_sync() submits T3 async work to the timeout pool WITHOUT propagating the OpenTelemetry context. Unlike _with_timeout() (line 194-201), which attaches the current context before executing the worker function, _run_sync() directly submits lambda: asyncio.run(coro) without context attachment. This causes spans created inside the Agent SDK T3 call to be orphaned from the active reasoning.complete span, breaking parent-child span relationships in Phoenix traces.
findings.best_practice_violated: OpenTelemetry context propagation across thread boundaries (OpenTelemetry spec: "context must be propagated when work moves between threads"). Mirrors the correct pattern already in place at line 194-201.
findings.proposed_fix: Propagate OTel context to the T3 worker thread by extracting the current context before submission and attaching it in the worker function, matching the pattern in _with_timeout(). Replace line 220 with context-aware submission that maintains span hierarchy.

findings.title: MLflow run-level run_id tagging not implemented
findings.problem: Graph runner functions (run_phase0_synthetic, resume_run, run_phase1_dry_run) call bootstrap_observability() and configure MLflow via configure_mlflow() BUT never call mlflow.start_run() or mlflow.set_tag() to tag the run with run_id. The mlflow_setup.py docstring documents that 'callers should tag every run with the platform run_id so it cross-references PostgresSaver/Phoenix/PROV-DM' via mlflow.set_tag("run_id", state["run_id"]) (line 20), and RUNBOOK.md documents querying by tags.run_id, but this tagging never occurs. MLflow experiment runs are created implicitly by the LangChain instrumentation but are not correlated to the graph run_id.
findings.best_practice_violated: Cross-store observability correlation (ADR-0015 #79). MLflow runs exist but are not tagged with the run_id, breaking the promised correlation pattern documented in RUNBOOK.md.
findings.proposed_fix: Wrap the graph.invoke() call in mlflow.start_run() and call mlflow.set_tag("run_id", state["run_id"]) after bootstrap_observability(). Ensure runs are properly closed on exit (use context manager). This should be done in all three graph runner functions.

findings.title: Missing OpenInference input/output MIME type attributes on LLM spans
findings.problem: The reasoning.complete and reasoning.complete_json spans set input.value and output.value but omit input.mime_type and output.mime_type attributes. Per OpenInference spec, MIME types should be declared to enable trace tooling to properly render and search text content. For text-only prompts and responses, these should be 'text/plain'.
findings.proposed_fix: Add span.set_attribute("input.mime_type", "text/plain") after line 561 and span.set_attribute("output.mime_type", "text/plain") after line 578 in complete(). Add the same to complete_json span at lines ~650-657.

findings.title: complete_json span lacks comprehensive LLM metadata attributes
findings.problem: The reasoning.complete_json span only sets 3 attributes (hcgrc.tier, hcgrc.run_id, hcgrc.agent_id) before delegating to complete(). While complete() has its own full span, complete_json's parent span is missing: openinference.span.kind, llm.provider, llm.model_name (initial/final), llm.temperature, llm.system, input.value, output.value, llm.finish_reason, llm.cost_usd, llm.retries. These make debugging JSON parse failures vs. backend failures harder — the parse-loop span is bare.
findings.best_practice_violated: OpenInference LLM span conventions and span completeness (each span should be self-descriptive of the operation it records)
findings.proposed_fix: Mirror complete()'s span setup in complete_json: add openinference.span.kind="LLM", llm.provider, known_model, llm.temperature, llm.system (if provided), input.value, and output.value on success. This makes the JSON parsing context visible without reading child spans.

result.title: Silent parameter contract violation in _attempt routing
result.verification:
Verified at src/reasoning_client.py:459-463. The candidate correctly identifies that json_mode is not passed to _t3_complete(). However, this is NOT a real defect:

1. _t3_complete() function signature (line 426) does not accept a json_mode parameter - it never needs it.

2. Design is intentional: T2 (Ollama) uses native JSON mode constrained decoding (line 249: format="json"), while T3 (Claude Agent SDK) uses prompt instructions instead (lines 634-637: "Respond with ONLY a single valid JSON...").

3. When complete_json() is called, it modifies the system prompt to include JSON instructions BEFORE calling complete() for any tier (line 634-637), so T3 receives the constraint via system prompt, not via json_mode parameter.

4. Balanced-brace extraction in _extract_json() (lines 700-719) handles JSON recovery for both tiers regardless of json_mode.

5. The parameter is silently ignored, but this is a tier-routing asymmetry (T2-only feature), not a functional bug. No edge cases or contract violations cause failures.

Already-mitigated: The JSON constraint for T3 is applied via modified system prompt in complete_json, making the json_mode parameter unnecessary.
result.recommended_action: WONTFIX - Not a real defect. The current implementation is correct: T3 doesn't support constrained JSON decoding and doesn't need json_mode. If documentation is desired, add a comment on line 463 explaining that T3 relies on prompt instructions and balanced-brace extraction instead of Ollama-style constrained decoding. Consider updating the _attempt() or complete() docstring to clarify that _json_mode is T2-specific, but this is not necessary for correctness.

result.title: Incorrect advisory lock release semantics in comment
result.verification: Confirmed in /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/checkpointer.py:229-231. The comment states "The advisory lock is session-scoped and auto-releases when the connection returns to the pool, so swallowing here is safe." This is factually incorrect about PostgreSQL semantics: advisory locks persist when connections are pooled (the session continues) and do NOT auto-release until explicitly released via pg_advisory_unlock() or the connection closes. The pg_advisory_unlock() call on line 233 is wrapped in a try/except that swallows all exceptions (line 234-235), so if it fails, the lock remains held. However, the actual runtime impact is mitigated by the architectural design: (1) setup() runs only once per process per conn_str, guarded by _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS (line 54, 222); (2) nobody else attempts to acquire the same MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY (verified by grep across codebase); (3) the pooled connections are only used for normal PostgresSaver operations after initialization, not for the advisory lock. Therefore, even if pg_advisory_unlock() fails and the lock persists, it does not cause a blocking scenario in practice. The factually incorrect comment was NOT fixed in Pass 7 batch 18 (commit da3d7a8), which only addressed the exception-masking issue (preventing unlock exceptions from masking setup() exceptions), not the comment's semantic incorrectness.
result.recommended_action: Update the comment to accurately document PostgreSQL advisory lock semantics and the actual failure scenario. Suggested replacement: "The advisory lock persists when the connection is returned to the pool because the session continues. If pg_advisory_unlock() fails (rare), the lock remains held on that connection. This is acceptable because: (1) unlock failures are extremely rare, (2) setup() runs once per process per conn_str (line 222 guard), (3) no subsequent code tries to acquire this lock, and (4) the lock eventually releases when the connection closes. However, future maintainers should be aware that if this code is refactored to allow re-initialization or if multiple threads attempt to acquire the same lock during initialization, a deadlock could occur."

result.title: Language identifier fence stripping matches JSON-variant prefixes incorrectly
result.verification:
CONFIRMED via code analysis and testing:

1. Code location verified: /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py:706
   - Line 706: `if inner[:4].lower() == "json":` — this is a PREFIX match, not exact match
   - Line 707: `inner = inner[4:]` — strips exactly 4 characters

2. Defect confirmed: The condition incorrectly matches JSON-variant language identifiers:
   - `json5` matches: inner[:4] = "json", leaves "5\n..." as remainder
   - `jsonl` matches: inner[:4] = "json", leaves "l\n..." as remainder
   - `jsonc` matches: inner[:4] = "json", leaves "c\n..." as remainder
   - `jsonschema` matches: inner[:4] = "json", leaves "schema\n..." as remainder

3. Reachability: REACHABLE
   - The code path executes when _extract_json() is called with a fence
   - _extract_json() is called from complete_json() at line 685
   - complete_json() is the public API for JSON extraction
   - An LLM could produce ```json5 or other variants in principle

4. Mitigation assessment: PARTIALLY MITIGATED (not complete)
   - Fallback mechanism exists (lines 713-718):
     - First tries `json.loads(candidate)` which fails for malformed prefixes
     - Falls back to `_first_balanced_json()` which extracts the first balanced {...} or [...]
   - For standard JSON-variant identifiers (json5, jsonl, jsonc): fallback DOES work
     - Tested: ```json5\n{"a": 1}\n``` → candidate becomes "5\n{"a": 1}" → fallback extracts {"a": 1}
     - Because the remnants (5, l, c, schema) don't contain braces
   - For edge case identifiers with braces: fallback FAILS
     - Tested: ```json{x}\n{"a": 1}\n``` → candidate becomes "{x}\n{"a": 1}" → fallback extracts {x} → JSON parse fails

5. No test coverage: The test suite (test_reasoning_client.py:37-46) does NOT cover JSON-variant fence identifiers. Only standard ```json is tested.

6. Severity rationale: MEDIUM (not high because fallback partially mitigates for common variants, not critical because unlikely to occur with well-trained Claude models in practice)
result.recommended_action: Apply the proposed fix. Change line 706 from `if inner[:4].lower() == "json":` to `if inner.lower().startswith("json") and (len(inner) == 4 or inner[4] in '\n\r \t'):` to enforce word boundary separation. This ensures exact match of "json" followed by whitespace or end-of-string, eliminating false positives on json5, jsonl, jsonc, jsonschema, and other variants. Add test cases covering json5, jsonl, jsonc, jsonschema variants to prevent regression.

result.title: OpenTelemetry context not propagated in _run_sync pool submission
result.verification: Verified at /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py:210-220. The _run_sync function detects running event loops with asyncio.get_running_loop() (line 213) and submits work to _submit_bounded() at line 220 using a bare lambda: `lambda: asyncio.run(coro)`. This contrasts sharply with _with_timeout (lines 185-201), which explicitly captures the current OTel context at line 194 (`ctx = otel_context.get_current()`) and attaches it in the worker thread at line 197 (`token = otel_context.attach(ctx)`). The _run_sync path lacks this propagation entirely. The code path is reachable: asyncio.get_running_loop() succeeds when code executes inside an active event loop (confirmed with asyncio.run(test())), and the test at test_reasoning_client.py:297-303 explicitly tests this branch via monkeypatch. No existing tests verify context propagation for this path. The impact is real: T3 spans from async contexts would lose parent span nesting in OpenTelemetry traces, orphaning them and breaking trace correlation. No mitigations exist elsewhere in the code.
result.recommended_action: Apply context propagation to _run_sync matching the _with_timeout pattern: capture otel_context.get_current() before line 220, then wrap the lambda to attach/detach context in the worker thread before and after asyncio.run(coro). Add a test to verify context propagation in the "running loop" path (similar to test_run_sync_with_running_loop but checking trace context nesting).

result.title: Race condition in instrument_langchain() — non-atomic check-and-set on _PROVIDER
result.verification:
Read /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/infrastructure/observability/phoenix_setup.py lines 86-144 (instrument_langchain function). Confirmed the check-then-set pattern exists at lines 96-98 and 143, with no locking mechanism. However, verified that the race condition is NOT reachable in practice:

1. instrument_langchain() is only called via bootstrap_observability() at /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/graph.py lines 172, 206, and 230 - all on the main thread BEFORE graph.invoke() runs
2. No concurrent thread creation occurs before bootstrap_observability() completes
3. ThreadPoolExecutor in reasoning_client (/Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py lines 129-131) is only invoked INSIDE graph nodes, not during bootstrap
4. No test coverage exists for concurrent instrument_langchain() calls (verified /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/tests/test_observability.py has idempotency test on line 56 but only tests single-threaded cache hit)
5. Codebase demonstrates thread-safety awareness elsewhere (checkpointer.py lines 35-41 use threading.Lock for similar check-and-set, and phoenix_setup.py line 153 checks threading.current_thread() for signal handler safety)

The execution order is always: bootstrap_observability() [MAIN] → instrument_langchain() [MAIN] → _PROVIDER set [MAIN] → graph.invoke() [may spawn workers]. Workers only call reasoning_client code, not bootstrap code. Therefore, the defect exists in the code but is unreachable given the application's architecture.
result.recommended_action: While the race condition is unreachable in the current architecture, this is a latent defect that could manifest if future code changes the initialization order. The recommended action is DEFENSIVE: add thread-safe locking following the pattern already established in checkpointer.py. Add `_provider_lock = threading.Lock()` at module level and wrap lines 96-144 with `with _provider_lock:` to make the check-register-cache operation atomic. This costs nothing at runtime (only the initial call ever contends the lock) and protects against future refactoring that might call instrument_langchain() from concurrent threads. The codebase already demonstrates this discipline (checkpointer.py:41 for PostgresSaver init), making this a consistency/hygiene improvement rather than a critical fix.

result.title: Gate 3 lacks explicit prerequisite validation despite docstring claiming "hard prerequisite"
result.verification:
Verified via direct code inspection:

DOCSTRING CLAIM (gates.py:315-316):
"Hard prerequisite: exploratory_complete must be True (or all agents in stub_pending status is acceptable for a governance dry-run)."

GATE 2 PATTERN (gates.py:228-272):
- Explicitly validates data_split_verified, exploratory_complete, hypothesis_set
- Lines 229-236: if conditions not met, appends failure_event
- Lines 269-271: writes deferred gate_status via gate_coordinator_node
- Line 272: returns early without calling _run_gate()
- Line 297: only calls _run_gate() if ALL prerequisites pass

GATE 3 IMPLEMENTATION (gates.py:303-367):
- NO prerequisite validation before _run_gate()
- Line 354-365: immediately builds proposal
- Line 367: calls _run_gate(state, "gate_3", proposal)
- No guards, no checks, no early return

TEST EVIDENCE (test_gate_decisions.py:39, 44-50):
- gate_3_node is tested in isolated single-node graph
- Invoked with initial_state() which has exploratory_complete=False (state.py:191)
- Test PASSES without setting any prerequisites
- This demonstrates the guard is completely absent

REACHABILITY ANALYSIS:
- Phase 0/1: Gate 3 is not wired in graph.py (comments at lines 24-26, 107-109)
- Phase 2: Not yet implemented; gates.py says they're in Phase 2 but not registered (graph.py:24-26)
- Current data flow: exploratory_phase_node → hypothesis_formalize_node → gate_2_node → [GATE 2 prerequisite check] → confirmatory_entry_node → [Phase 2, not yet implemented]
- Gate 2 validates exploratory_complete is True (line 231); if False, defers (line 270)
- By design, if flow reaches Phase 2, exploratory_complete MUST be True due to Gate 2's check

MITIGATION STATUS:
- Idempotency guard in _run_gate() (line 135) doesn't help — only guards against re-firing after approval
- No defensive check at routing level prevents reaching Gate 3 without exploratory_complete
- Normal execution flow is currently SAFE due to Gate 2's checks, but Gate 3 is not defensively guarded
- Inconsistent with Gate 2's explicit validation pattern
result.recommended_action: Add explicit prerequisite validation to gate_3_node matching the pattern from gate_2_node (gates.py:228-272). Before building the proposal at line 354, insert a block that: (1) checks if exploratory_complete is True, OR if all agents in eda_agent_statuses have "stub_pending" status (dry-run case); (2) if prerequisites unmet, write a deferred gate_status record via gate_coordinator_node and return early; (3) append a gate_prerequisite_failure event (similar to gate_2_node lines 259-267) if not already recorded. This ensures the docstring's claim of a "hard prerequisite" is actually enforced in code, providing defensive guarantees if Phase 2 is wired incorrectly or if gate_3_node is called from an unexpected entry point.

findings.title: SIGTERM handler body untested (missing invocation)
findings.problem: The _handler function registered by _install_sigterm_flush() is never invoked in tests. This critical failure path must call provider.force_flush(), conditionally provider.shutdown(), and sys.exit(0), but test_install_sigterm_flush_registers_on_main_thread only verifies the handler is registered and callable, not that the body executes correctly or handles exceptions.
findings.best_practice_violated: Test coverage of failure/recovery branches: the SIGTERM flush path is essential for graceful shutdown under SIGTERM (launchd/kill/container-stop) but is untested.
findings.proposed_fix: Add test_sigterm_handler_calls_force_flush_and_shutdown() that invokes the captured handler and verifies: (1) provider.force_flush() is called, (2) provider.shutdown() is called if it exists, (3) sys.exit(0) is invoked. Use monkeypatch to capture sys.exit and verify its call.

findings.title: ChatOllama socket timeout exception handling not tested
findings.problem: When ChatOllama.invoke() raises a socket timeout (socket.timeout -> TimeoutError), the code catches it in the except Exception handler (line 290) and converts to ReasoningError for retry. However, there is no test that injects a socket timeout to verify this path works. The critical timeout margin (T2_TIMEOUT + 10 outer vs. T2_TIMEOUT inner HTTP timeout) is designed so the HTTP timeout fires first, but the recovery behavior is untested.
findings.best_practice_violated: Test coverage of failure/recovery branches: socket timeouts are a common failure mode for HTTP clients but have no coverage.
findings.proposed_fix: Add test_t2_socket_timeout_triggers_retry() that mocks ChatOllama.invoke() to raise socket.timeout, and verify it is caught, wrapped as ReasoningError, and retried (not treated as permanent).

findings.title: ChatOllama json_mode branch not exercised in tests
findings.problem: The _ollama() function's json_mode branch (kwargs['format'] = 'json') is used by complete_json() calls, but all tests mock _attempt() instead of exercising the real path. The json_mode=True case creates ChatOllama with format=json, which is never instantiated in tests.
findings.best_practice_violated: Test coverage: the native JSON mode for Ollama is a critical feature (#159, #175) but has zero path coverage.
findings.proposed_fix: Add test_t2_json_mode_configures_format(monkeypatch) that doesn't mock _attempt, instead patches ChatOllama.__init__ to capture kwargs and verify format='json' is set when json_mode=True.

findings.title: T2_TIMEOUT + 10 margin invariant not validated
findings.problem: The code comment states 'Give the outer future timeout margin over the inner HTTP timeout so the inner socket timeout wins' but there is no test validating this critical invariant. The margin exists (T2_TIMEOUT + 10 vs. T2_TIMEOUT), but the behavior when the inner timeout fires first is not tested (see socket timeout issue above).
findings.best_practice_violated: Test coverage of timeout ordering: the timeout margin is a correctness property (prevents worker abandonment) but has no validation test.
findings.proposed_fix: Add test_t2_timeout_margin_ensures_inner_fires_first(monkeypatch) that verifies T2_TIMEOUT < T2_TIMEOUT + 10 and documents the intended timeout ordering. Combined with the socket timeout test above, this validates the margin is effective.

findings.title: Pool slot release on exception not fully covered
findings.problem: test_submit_bounded_releases_slot_on_completion uses a polling loop with real time.sleep(0.01) waits, which can be racy if the done callback is delayed. While the 2-second deadline makes it unlikely to fail in practice, the callback mechanics under exception propagation are not explicitly tested.
findings.best_practice_violated: Test reliability: polling with real sleeps can introduce non-determinism.
findings.proposed_fix: Use a threading.Event instead of polling to synchronously verify the done callback fires when the future completes. Example: set an Event in the callback, then assert the event is set after future.result().

result.title: MLflow run-level run_id tagging not implemented in graph runners
result.verification:
Confirmed by code inspection:

1. /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/graph.py:172 - run_phase0_synthetic() calls bootstrap_observability() but never calls mlflow.start_run() or mlflow.set_tag()
2. /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/graph.py:180 - graph.invoke() wrapped in run_trace_context() (OTel context only, no MLflow)
3. /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/graph.py:206 - resume_run() same pattern: bootstrap_observability() + run_trace_context()
4. /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/graph.py:230 - run_phase1_dry_run() same pattern

Verified documentation requirements:
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/infrastructure/tracking/mlflow_setup.py:18-20 docstring explicitly documents: "callers should tag every run with the platform run_id... mlflow.set_tag("run_id", state["run_id"])"
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/docs/architecture/RUNBOOK.md:143 specifies querying by tags.run_id
- /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/docs/decisions/ADR-0015-architecture-integrity-decisions.md:136 decision #79 specifies: "MLflow | run_id as experiment tag"

No mitigation found - mlflow.set_tag() and mlflow.start_run() are never called in the runner functions or in run_trace_context(). The defect is real and reachable on every invocation of the three graph runner functions.
result.recommended_action: Wrap graph.invoke() calls in all three runner functions with mlflow.start_run() context manager and call mlflow.set_tag("run_id", state["run_id"]) after bootstrap_observability(). Example pattern: with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.set_tag("run_id", state["run_id"]); with run_trace_context(state["run_id"]): result = graph.invoke(...) Ensure runs are properly closed on context exit.

result.title: T3 timeout thread loses OTel trace context
result.verification:
Confirmed by code inspection. File /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py:
- Line 220: _run_sync() submits lambda: asyncio.run(coro) to thread pool WITHOUT context propagation
- Lines 194-201: _with_timeout() DOES propagate context via otel_context.get_current() and otel_context.attach()
- Call path: complete() (line 540 starts reasoning.complete span) -> _attempt() -> _t3_complete() -> _run_sync() (line 429)
- At line 220, OTel context IS active (we're inside the reasoning.complete span), but it is NOT attached to the worker function
- Unlike T2 which was fixed in commit c6b8de8 for issue #238 (which modified _with_timeout to propagate context), T3 via _run_sync was never modified
- The defect is reachable when T3 tier is available and complete() is called within an active event loop (line 213-214 branch taken)
- Code comments at line 543-544 confirm "T3 has no LangChain span" but do not address whether Agent SDK emits OTel spans
- This is a gap in coverage: issue #238 fixed context propagation for T2 but not for T3, despite both using the same thread pool mechanism
result.recommended_action: Apply the same context-propagation pattern from _with_timeout() (lines 194-201) to _run_sync(). Extract the OTel context before submission and attach it in the worker lambda, matching the established pattern. This will ensure T3 spans nest correctly under reasoning.complete in Phoenix, maintaining span hierarchy consistency between T2 and T3 tiers.

findings.title: pytest-asyncio missing from pyproject.toml [dev] optional dependencies
findings.problem: pytest-asyncio is pinned in requirements.txt (line 102: 'pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7' with no upper bound), but is not listed in pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies] dev array. The comment at requirements.txt:98 explicitly states 'Bounded floors (not exact ==) to match requirements-ci.txt and pyproject [dev], so all three install the same major and CI exercises what the node runs (pass-11).' This is a parity violation: a developer running 'pip install -e ".[dev]"' will not constrain pytest-asyncio to a major version, potentially installing an incompatible future version (e.g., 2.0+) that differs from the production venv (currently 1.4.0) and what CI tests against.
findings.best_practice_violated: Dependency consistency across dev/CI/prod environments; pass-11 hardening requirement for major-version parity
findings.proposed_fix: Add 'pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7,<2' to pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies] dev array (line 45, after pytest-cov). Also add an upper bound to requirements.txt line 102 to match: 'pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7,<2'.

findings.title: pytest-asyncio is in requirements.txt but missing from requirements-ci.txt (parity violation)
findings.problem: pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7 is declared in requirements.txt for production use, but is entirely absent from requirements-ci.txt. This means CI tests do not exercise the same installed major version as the production node, violating the parity principle stated at requirements.txt:98. Either pytest-asyncio is Phase 1-only (and should be removed from base requirements.txt or marked as optional), or it should be in CI to maintain parity. The unbounded floor (no <N ceiling) in requirements.txt also means future major versions are implicitly allowed.
findings.best_practice_violated: CI/prod parity principle (pass-2 #191); documented consistency requirement at requirements.txt:98-99
findings.proposed_fix: Either (a) remove pytest-asyncio from requirements.txt if it is Phase 1-only, or (b) add 'pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7,<2' to requirements-ci.txt for full parity. Update requirements.txt line 102 to include an upper bound: 'pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7,<2'.

result.title: Missing OpenInference input/output MIME type attributes on LLM spans
result.verification: Examined /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py lines 561, 578 (complete function) and 649-657 (complete_json function). The code does set input.value and output.value attributes correctly. However, the OpenInference specification (as defined in /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages/openinference/semconv/trace/__init__.py lines 8-17) explicitly states "If unspecified, the type is plain text by default" for both input.mime_type and output.mime_type. The OpenInference instrumentation library's own implementation (get_input_attributes/get_output_attributes in _attributes.py) only sets MIME type attributes if explicitly provided. Since both the prompt and response are strings, plain text is the correct default per spec. complete_json() correctly does not set input/output values on its own span; it delegates to complete() which creates a child span with these attributes. This is not a defect but rather an optional observability enhancement that already has a defined default behavior in the spec.
result.recommended_action: No action required. The code correctly follows the OpenInference specification, which makes MIME type attributes optional with plain text as the documented default. If trace tooling completeness is a priority, adding explicit mime_type attributes would be a nice-to-have enhancement rather than a defect fix. The current implementation is spec-compliant and functionally correct.

result.title: complete_json span lacks comprehensive LLM metadata attributes
result.verification: Read /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py:649-697. The complete_json parent span sets: hcgrc.tier, hcgrc.run_id (pass-6), session.id (pass-6), hcgrc.agent_id (pass-5), json.parse_attempts (pass-10), plus exception recording on failure. The candidate claims missing OpenInference attributes (openinference.span.kind, llm.provider, llm.model_name, llm.temperature, llm.system, input.value, output.value, llm.finish_reason, llm.cost_usd, llm.retries). However: (1) Line 647-648 comment explicitly states the span purpose: "Span the parse-retry loop so bad-JSON regenerations are visible in Phoenix rather than silent (#269)". (2) Each invocation of complete() at line 671 creates a child span with ALL these LLM attributes (verified lines 540-579). (3) This design—parent span tracking the retry loop, child spans carrying LLM context—has survived 11 hardening passes with adversarial review. (4) Pass-10 audited the complete_json span (batch 21) and added json.parse_attempts on failure path, finding it adequate. (5) Pass-11 found no issues with complete_json span structure. (6) This is an intentional architectural choice, not a defect: parent spans should not duplicate child span data. (7) The failure path IS reachable (line 688-691) and is properly recorded with span.record_exception() and span.set_status() (696). Debuggers can examine child spans for full LLM context. This is already-mitigated by design and multiple passes of validation.
result.recommended_action: REJECT. This is a false positive / design choice already validated. The complete_json parent span is intentionally minimal (tracking the JSON parse-retry loop only) while delegating LLM metadata to child complete() spans. The architecture is sound: tracing hierarchy shows correlation IDs on parent, full LLM context on children. No code change needed. If span self-description is desired (for observability tooling that doesn't drill into children), that would be a future enhancement, not a defect.

result.title: SIGTERM handler body untested (missing invocation)
result.verification: Read /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/infrastructure/observability/phoenix_setup.py:147-168 (the _install_sigterm_flush function and _handler closure at lines 156-162 that executes provider.force_flush(), conditionally provider.shutdown(), and sys.exit(0)). Read /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/tests/test_observability.py:105-117 (test_install_sigterm_flush_registers_on_main_thread). The test captures the handler and verifies it is registered with signal.SIGTERM and is callable, but never invokes the handler function to test its body execution. Confirmed no other test files invoke the handler (grep -rn "_handler" /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/tests/ returned only the single assertion at line 117). The handler is critical because: (1) atexit does NOT run on SIGTERM per line 136, making this the only graceful shutdown path for killed runs; (2) data loss documented at line 138 "the tail of a killed run is silently lost"; (3) Exception handling and conditional shutdown() invocation are untested; (4) Production paths include container stop and launchd kill signals. This is not a false positive or already-mitigated — the handler is registered but its body is never invoked in any test.
result.recommended_action: Add test_sigterm_handler_invocation (or similar) that: (1) captures the handler from _install_sigterm_flush(), (2) mocks provider.force_flush() and provider.shutdown() to track calls, (3) mocks sys.exit() to capture the exit call, (4) invokes the captured handler with mock signal and frame arguments, (5) asserts force_flush() was called, (6) asserts shutdown() was called (if present), (7) asserts sys.exit(0) was invoked. Additionally add a test for exception handling that verifies sys.exit(0) is still called even if force_flush() raises.

result.title: pytest-asyncio is in requirements.txt but missing from requirements-ci.txt (parity violation)
result.verification: Verified against /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/requirements.txt:102 (contains pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7), /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/requirements-ci.txt (no pytest-asyncio present), and /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/pyproject.toml lines 37-46 (dev dependencies do not include pytest-asyncio). The parity principle is documented in requirements.txt:98-99: "Bounded floors (not exact ==) to match requirements-ci.txt and pyproject [dev], so all three install the same major and CI exercises what the node runs (pass-11)." Confirmed via grep -r that pytest-asyncio is never used: no @pytest.mark.asyncio decorators on test functions exist (grep found zero results), and the async def coroutines in test_reasoning_client.py (lines 292, 301, 314, 345, 593) are internal helper functions called from synchronous test functions, not pytest async tests. No pytest.ini_options configure asyncio mode. This is a real parity violation: CI does not have pytest-asyncio installed while production does, and the documented principle explicitly requires matching major versions across all three dependency sources (pass-11 enforcement). The unbounded floor (>=0.23.7 with no <N ceiling) is a separate issue from the candidate's claim.
result.recommended_action: This is a real defect but the fix depends on intent: (1) If pytest-asyncio is genuinely unused (confirmed), remove it from requirements.txt line 102, matching the removal from requirements-ci.txt and pyproject.toml—since no async tests exist and none are planned. (2) Alternatively, if async testing is planned for future phases, add pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7,<1 to requirements-ci.txt and update pyproject.toml [dev] to match, restoring parity. Recommend option 1 (removal) since zero evidence of async test usage exists in the codebase. Add an upper bound to any remaining test dependencies: if kept, change to pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7,<1 to bound the major version.

result.title: Pool slot release on exception not fully covered
result.verification:
Verified via code inspection and testing:

1. Test suite examination (tests/test_reasoning_client.py):
   - test_submit_bounded_releases_slot_on_completion (line 228-237): Tests normal completion only (lambda: 7), uses polling with 0.01s sleeps
   - test_submit_bounded_decrements_on_submit_failure (line 276-288): Tests submit() failure
   - test_with_timeout_raises_on_overrun (line 175-178): Tests timeout exception but doesn't verify slot release
   - NO test exists for: future completing with an exception raised in the worker function

2. Implementation review (src/reasoning_client.py line 146-179):
   - _submit_bounded correctly adds a done callback (_release) that decrements _inflight
   - Handles submit() failure with try/except (line 172-177)
   - Callback is added only after successful submit (line 178)

3. Python concurrency behavior verified:
   - concurrent.futures.Future.add_done_callback() fires when future is done, regardless of result/exception
   - Tested: callbacks fire identically for success case, exception case, and timeout case
   - No behavioral difference for exceptions vs. normal completion

4. Polling raciness verified:
   - test_submit_bounded_releases_slot_on_completion uses time.sleep(0.01) polling
   - Lambda: 7 completes in microseconds, callback fires immediately after
   - 2-second timeout provides 200x margin
   - Practically very safe, but theoretically could race under extreme load

FINDING IS VALID but already MITIGATED BY CORRECT IMPLEMENTATION:
- Code correctly handles exceptions (callback fires regardless)
- TEST COVERAGE GAP: no explicit test for exception case completion
- POLLING ISSUE: minor - polling works but is not ideal test design
result.recommended_action:
Add a test for slot release when future completes with exception, and optionally improve polling to use threading.Event for determinism. Test like:
```python
def test_submit_bounded_releases_slot_on_exception(monkeypatch):
    # Verify slot is released when worker function raises
    import time
    monkeypatch.setattr(rc, "_inflight", 0)
    fut = rc._submit_bounded(lambda: 1/0)
    try:
        fut.result(timeout=2)
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        pass
    deadline = time.monotonic() + 2
    while rc._inflight != 0 and time.monotonic() < deadline:
        time.sleep(0.01)
    assert rc._inflight == 0
```

The actual mechanism works correctly; this is a test coverage/best-practice improvement, not a bug fix.

result.title: Hard-prerequisite deferred path cannot be resumed via resume_run()
result.verification:
Confirmed via code review and practical testing:

CODE EVIDENCE (src/nodes/gates.py:238-272):
- When hard prerequisites fail, gate_2_node writes a deferred decision to gate_status but returns WITHOUT calling interrupt() (the only source of interrupt is in _run_gate, which this path bypasses entirely)
- Lines 246-247 comment: "deferred is non-terminal, so this path re-runs whenever gate_2 is re-entered...while prereqs are still unmet" and line 244 says "The operator re-triggers once prereqs are met"
- BUT: there is no code implementing this re-trigger mechanism

PRACTICAL VERIFICATION:
1. When I invoked the graph to gate_2 with unmet prerequisites: gate_2_node() executed the hard-failure path (lines 238-272), wrote deferred decision, and returned. The graph completed normally to END with no parked interrupt (parked.next == ())

2. When I called resume_run("gate_2") after hard-prereq deferred: the operator's "approved" decision was NOT recorded. The gate_status['gate_2']['decision'] remained 'deferred' (from the system, not the operator), proving the resume was a no-op

3. The resume failure occurred because LangGraph's resume mechanism requires a parked interrupt() to resume from. Since no interrupt() was ever fired for the hard-prereq path, there is nothing to resume.

DESIGN INTENT vs REALITY:
- ADR-0015 (#80) and gates.py comments assume operator recovery via "re-triggering"
- Per ADR-0014, gate decisions flow through the interrupt/resume channel
- Currently, hard-prereq deferred decisions bypass that channel entirely
- No documented path exists for operator to provide a decision when prerequisites are later met

MITIGATION CHECK:
- No idempotency guards, timeouts, or validation reducers mitigate this
- The failure is structural: the hard-prereq path simply does not fire interrupt()
- The comment at line 244 describing the recovery mechanism is aspirational but unimplemented
result.recommended_action: Gate 2's hard-prerequisite path must fire an interrupt() before deferring, allowing the operator to acknowledge the failure. Currently line 244's comment says "The operator re-triggers once prereqs are met" but provides no mechanism. Two options: (1) Refactor the hard-prereq path to call _run_gate() with a non-approvable proposal that presents the hard-failure rationale, similar to validation errors in _run_gate lines 146-148 (this gives a proper recovery path where the operator can decline and retry later); (2) Create an explicit resume_run-compatible interrupt point in the hard-prereq path that parks the run, allowing the operator to acknowledge the failure and retry via resume_run when prerequisites are met. Option 1 is preferred as it reuses existing interrupt/resume machinery.

result.title: T2_TIMEOUT + 10 margin invariant not tested
result.verification: Verified in /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py:241 (inner timeout `client_kwargs: {"timeout": T2_TIMEOUT}`) vs line 279 (outer timeout `T2_TIMEOUT + 10`). Comment at lines 274-277 documents the intent: the margin ensures the inner socket timeout fires first to prevent worker abandonment. Test file /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/tests/test_reasoning_client.py line 175 has `test_with_timeout_raises_on_overrun()` which tests that timeouts raise, but there is NO test validating: (1) the T2_TIMEOUT < T2_TIMEOUT + 10 relationship, (2) that an inner socket timeout fires before the outer one, or (3) that the margin prevents worker abandonment. The invariant is implemented correctly (hardcoded +10 margin exists), but the critical timeout ordering is NOT explicitly validated by any test.
result.recommended_action: Add test_t2_timeout_margin_ensures_inner_fires_first() that (1) validates T2_TIMEOUT < T2_TIMEOUT + 10 as an invariant, (2) documents why the margin exists (worker abandonment prevention per #272), and (3) simulates an inner HTTP socket timeout with a mock to verify it fires before the outer future timeout. This closes the test coverage gap on a critical correctness property. The actual implementation is sound (margin is applied), but lack of explicit validation makes the invariant fragile to future changes.

result.title: pytest-asyncio missing from pyproject.toml [dev] optional dependencies
result.verification:
CONFIRMED DEFECT with MITIGATED severity:

FILE VERIFICATION:
- pyproject.toml:34-46 [project.optional-dependencies] dev: Contains only pytest>=8.2.2,<9; pytest-cov>=5.0.0,<6; ruff; black; pre-commit. NO pytest-asyncio. ✓
- requirements.txt:102: `pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7` (NO upper bound). ✓
- requirements-ci.txt:1-33: Zero mention of pytest-asyncio. ✓
- Pass-11 commit message (6debed1): "Bounded floors (not exact ==) to match requirements-ci.txt and pyproject [dev], so all three install the same major" — applied ONLY to pytest and pytest-cov, not pytest-asyncio. ✓

PARITY VIOLATION CONFIRMED:
- The pass-11 comment at requirements.txt:98-99 claims to align three files for "major-version parity" but pytest-asyncio was NOT included in that alignment.
- `pip install -e ".[dev]"` installs pytest (bounded, <9), pytest-cov (bounded, <6), but NOT pytest-asyncio at all.
- `pip install -r requirements.txt` installs pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7 (unbounded, could resolve to 2.0+).
- CI (requirements-ci.txt) does NOT install pytest-asyncio.

REACHABILITY & ACTUAL IMPACT (MITIGATING FACTORS):
- pytest-asyncio provides @pytest.mark.asyncio decorator for async test functions.
- The test suite has ZERO async test functions (all tests are synchronous).
- reasoning_client.py uses asyncio internally, but that does NOT require pytest-asyncio.
- The unbounded pytest-asyncio is installed but NOT USED in the current test suite.
- No failure path exists for current tests: the decorator is never invoked.

SEVERITY RATIONALE:
- Originally HIGH (candidate): assumes pytest-asyncio is actively used in tests. ✓ Verified UNUSED.
- Adjusted to MEDIUM: Technical parity violation exists (inconsistency across three files and potential future breakage when async tests are added), but zero impact on current codebase. A developer adding async tests in the future would encounter the issue (pytest-asyncio would be missing from `pip install -e ".[dev]"`), making this a forward-facing risk rather than immediate.
result.recommended_action:
1. Add `pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7,<2` to pyproject.toml line 45 (after pytest-cov) to match the floor in requirements.txt.
2. Add upper bound to requirements.txt line 102: change `pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7` to `pytest-asyncio>=0.23.7,<2` (aligns with pass-11 principle of major-version bounding).
3. Optionally add pytest-asyncio to requirements-ci.txt if async tests are planned; otherwise, leave it out and do NOT add to pyproject since it's only needed for development of async tests (not currently present).
4. Update the comment at requirements.txt:98 to clarify which tools are aligned: "pytest, pytest-cov are bounded to match requirements-ci.txt and pyproject [dev]."

result.title: ChatOllama socket timeout exception handling not tested
result.verification: Verified in /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py lines 273-297. The code correctly catches socket.timeout via the broad `except Exception` on line 290 and converts it to ReasoningError for retry on line 297. However, no test exists that injects socket.timeout into ChatOllama.invoke() to verify this path. Searched test_reasoning_client.py and found 0 matches for "socket" and 0 uses of "socket.timeout". The only test for exception handling on invoke() is test_t2_classifies_missing_model_as_permanent which tests RuntimeError with specific message patterns. The timeout margin design (T2_TIMEOUT + 10 outer vs. T2_TIMEOUT inner) assumes the HTTP timeout fires first, making socket.timeout a theoretical but reachable edge case from httpx/requests low-level socket operations.
result.recommended_action: Add test_t2_socket_timeout_triggers_retry() that: (1) mocks ChatOllama to raise socket.timeout from invoke(), (2) verifies it is caught and wrapped as ReasoningError (not PermanentReasoningError), (3) verifies the retry loop in complete() actually retries on this exception. This validates the timeout margin recovery path works as designed.

result.title: ChatOllama json_mode branch not exercised in tests
result.verification: Verified in /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/src/reasoning_client.py:243-251: The json_mode branch sets kwargs["format"]="json" before instantiating ChatOllama. Traced the call chain: complete_json() at line 674 calls complete(..., _json_mode=True), which calls _attempt(..., json_mode) at line 570, which calls _t2_complete(..., json_mode) at line 462, which calls _ollama(..., json_mode) at line 261. The _ollama function at lines 243-249 checks json_mode and sets the format parameter. Examined all tests in /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/tests/test_reasoning_client.py: test_complete_json_parses_stubbed_reply (line 156) mocks _attempt; test_complete_json_exhausts_retries_then_raises (line 181) mocks _attempt; test_complete_json_varies_temperature_across_retries (line 199) mocks complete; test_complete_json_varies_temperature_with_high_input (line 240) mocks complete; test_complete_json_does_not_retry_backend_errors (line 259) mocks complete; test_complete_json_records_parse_attempts_on_failure (line 618) mocks _attempt; test_complete_json_sets_session_id_on_span (line 630) mocks complete. The only test touching _ollama is test_t2_classifies_missing_model_as_permanent (line 162) which mocks _ollama itself and passes json_mode=False. Confirmed: ChatOllama does support the format parameter (verified via introspection of installed langchain_ollama). The code is reachable and not mitigated elsewhere - it would execute if complete_json(Tier.T2, ...) is called. No integration/smoke tests exist in the repo (checked /Users/thomasjones/hc-grc/tests).
result.recommended_action: Add a unit test that doesn't mock _attempt or complete, instead patches ChatOllama.__init__ to capture and verify that format='json' is passed in kwargs when _ollama() is called with json_mode=True. The test should call _t2_complete(..., json_mode=True) or directly test _ollama(model, temp, True) to ensure the parameter propagates correctly through the stack. This provides direct coverage of the json_mode feature (#159, #175) at the unit level, complementing the documented (but absent) integration smoke tests.
